column2: — And Marta?
Won't she bolt?
— asked the grandmother.
— Instincts, Granny, may take over, perhaps.
— She has no instincts at all, except for hysterics, — the grandmother said sadly.

Yet Marta, with that same surprised face on the verge of squeamishness, took the children in her arms, barely pressed their noses, stroked their hedgehog heads with one finger, and her heart clenched when they cried.
She covered her ears and handed them to Fumitaka.
— Will you come to ours for dinner?
— Miyechka asked Ustym.
— I won't.
Then they went for a walk.
The roar receded, and they drew near to the pine forest, beyond which a marsh lived quietly, overgrown with sedge, in which vipers, grass snakes and all sorts of other vermin dwelt.
Evening was descending.

Miyechka could see that Ustym was out of sorts — maybe it was painful for him too, like for her, to look at the apple trees, or maybe he was musing over the fact that he himself would have lived here, the way the Japanese man was about to live, raising sons and plums.
From the expression on his face Miyechka couldn't lean toward either version, and there was no point in asking, because he never told her anything of the kind.
— To build a house by this sedge, — said Miyechka, — and every evening listen to the grass snakes and vipers crawling there and the frogs singing resonantly.
— All the pleasure of such an idea ends where, instead of dreams, reality begins, — retorted Ustym.

From that evening Miyechka remembered only two images — the apple trees crashing down, and the way Ustym blinked, trying to drive off the mosquitoes that flew out of the sedge for their evening hunt for hot blood.
He blinked his long lashes and spat comically, cursing the mosquitoes and calling them sons of bitches.
Not a single insect landed on the cold-blooded, tanned Miyechka.
They came out onto a rise, and from here the apple orchard looked like embroidery — two rows remained.
— And what will you do next?
— said Ustym, when they were already standing on the highway, knowing that they were about to drive off in different directions.
Here Miyechka should have told Ustym that Danylo always falls asleep in one position and every morning folds his socks — doesn't roll them up, but folds them.
And that he is calm and loves her.
That she sees it in his eyes, and that he gives her room to maneuver.

She should have said that she'd agreed simply because she wasn't used to acting off-script and this marriage looked entirely logical, but that she thought about him all the time and wanted more than anything in the world to be in Marta's place now — growing plums in this house, but with him, Ustym, instead of Fumitaka.
Instead Miyechka said:
— I don't know.
And then she got into the car and gripped the wheel the whole way home, periodically pounding it with her fist.
She knew the script perfectly and knew which act came next.
She didn't know how to get around it, and felt that she would never dare act against it.
In conversation with herself Miyechka admitted that her parents weren't forcing her, weren't marrying her off to anyone, that she owed no one anything, but the script still seemed carved in stone.
— The worst prison is your own self, — she said to herself, turning off the headlights in front of the gate.
She saw that someone outside had turned on the light, and lingered for a second by the gate.
The summer evening smelled the way evenings smell only in August, when the finiteness of summer can already be felt.

Miyechka stood before the gate, breathing and trying to calm herself before the children would rush at her, before Marta's twins would scream, before the grandmother would ask her to think up something for supper.

She listened to the silence, and then opened the wicket gate and resolutely stepped into the yard of the refuge, which had suddenly turned into the home of her childhood, where it was always noisy and crowded.
As she walked toward the house, it even seemed to her that the raspberry had retreated a few dozen centimeters from the threshold.
The children came running toward her.
The Harvest
Summer, meanwhile, was racing swiftly to its end, and all the hopes placed on its conclusion were gradually fading.
Miyechka stopped hoping that it, this three-month-old infant — at first damp, and then sweltering — would decide something for her.
Lilichka realized that nothing would become any clearer of its own accord for her either, even though she'd taken this three-month pause.
On the contrary — everything had only become more complicated: she now had Lusya.
And even though Marta had drawn the short match, it was still clear that Lusya, as it were, belonged to her.
The little girl had begun to talk more and was always carrying the kitten in her bosom, so her whole belly was scratched.
The kitten didn't yet know how to be gentle, the way children don't know how to be gentle, biting their mothers.
— Don't press Yulya to yourself, — the grandmother would beg Lusya.
— Don't put her against your bare skin.
— Co-o-old, — Lusya would say, and only in this house did they know what she meant, because she pronounced 'cold' in only two syllables — co and ld.
It was unclear whether she pitied Yulya, who had no fur, or was cold herself, because her skin positively glowed, so thin and transparent was she.

Nati was constantly clicking her tongue beside her, and Maria had to defend the little girl from her, since Nati kept trying to feed her biscuits made with lard, ice cream and simply plain chocolate, which she kept somewhere on her person.
It was cramped in the house.
Fumitaka with Marta, the chubby-cheeked Japanese boys and Lusya took up almost half the house.
Lilichka with Greg and the thin boys slept in one room.

Maria slept on the sofa with Nati, who was happy with this turn of events, and before finally releasing Maria into the embrace of sleep, would, for an hour or even more, retell her love stories, despite the fact that Maria understood at most about thirty percent.
— He was, — she'd say, pronouncing the English 'h' like the Spanish one, which had come to them from Arabic and, rasping, formed somewhere in the throat.
— Very big!
En todos los sentidos, mujer!
— she would then whisper and burst into hoarse laughter.
Nati always smelled of perfume and chocolate.
Maria tried to say nothing in reply, so as not to accidentally tug the thread of the next story, but she never managed it.
— You silence!

— Nati would whisper with strain and went on retelling about her taciturn lover, who by a strange coincidence was silent in exactly the way Maria was now silent, looking out the window, into which shone a full, white-yellow, cheddar-colored, mottled moon.
— Looks like cheddar, — said Maria.
— Que no!
— Nati shouted.
— I don't want to hear a single word about cheddar.
When they finally fell asleep, the moon already lit up the whole garden, and the leaves of the thickets in its light seemed gray-blue.
Miyechka slept outside, under the roof that had once served as the grandfather's workshop.

She had laid out grass for herself, put a mattress on top, covered herself with a snow-white blanket with little red flowers and, in the middle of the quiet night, when nothing stirred anywhere, read in snatches, trying to read a sentence quickly before the screen light faded.

As soon as the screen lit up, all sorts of vermin flew into Miyechka's face — moths with fat legs and shaggy cheeks, beetles with clinging little legs, almost inaudible mosquitoes and insects light as cobwebs with an endless number of legs.
It was quiet.
Only something rustled in the raspberry.
And cats walked about, setting their paws on the ground without a sound.
Miyechka thought that maybe the ginger cat, posthumously named Yakiv, also walked about here somewhere with them.
Maybe all the cats she'd buried in her childhood came here at night and roamed the raspberry, living out their eighth cat-life.
They harvested the pumpkins on the last day of summer.

— It's an early variety, and they'll ripen this way, — said Maria, who was an expert on everything that had roots and tubers and bore berries, fruits, icicle-shaped things of unclear origin and edible flowers.
It was the morning of a hot day.
Marta spread a large checked blanket under the apple tree, dangerous by the grandmother's scale, and laid the children there one by one.
The babies' cheeks caused a gravitational pull toward the earth, so the newborns could only kick their legs and faintly grunt.
— They don't look like our children at all, — grandmother Teodora had said, leaning over the boys.
— When do their eyes open, Marta?
— she asked.
Marta, kneeling before the children and fastening the snaps on their clothes, repeated:
— Granny, they can see, just like human children!
— But what can they see with?
— asked the grandmother.
— Their little eyes haven't even opened yet, have they?
Miyechka would try to stop the grandmother from these superstitions, but once, taking one of the twins in her arms, she quietly lifted his eyelid to check whether it really opened.
Under the eyelid was a black, attentive eye.
Fumitaka said that out of respect for this land on which his children were born, they would name one Petro, and the other — Pavlo.
— Pablo and Pedro!
— Nati threw up her hands on hearing the names.
The grandmother kept probing Marta about whether she'd pressured Fumitaka over the names, because she certainly could.
Marta waved it off.
For now it seemed to her altogether unimportant what they'd be called.
It seemed to Marta that they'd be helpless little lumps like this all their lives, and what difference did it make what they'd be called.
The realization that real people would grow from these children hadn't come to her yet.
She fed them matter-of-factly, like a cat, but loved her Lusya.
The latter nestled up to her all the time and kissed Petro and Pavlo's little feet.
— But they were born closer to Elijah's Day, — said Mrs. Darochka, on learning what the twins had been named.
— Elijah, is that the one who, into the water, you-know-what?
— Lilichka asked matter-of-factly.
— Him, — Mrs. Darochka retorted sharply, — but they're named after the apostles, you see.
It's pleasing to God.
While the newborn apostles lay under the apple tree, everyone capable of physical labor rolled the pumpkins in from the vegetable garden.
Only the grandmother sat on a chair by the apostles and drove off the bothersome, by now autumnal, flies.
Greg and Fumitaka could carry the pumpkins.
Greg picked up a pumpkin the way a normal person would.
Fumitaka, however, would squat down with a straight back, and then rise to his feet with a shout.
A little way off the poplars rustled, the first gossamer began to float, caterpillars crawled, flies bit and from time to time Fumitaka's shouts rang out.
The rest rolled the pumpkins.
Marko pushed Roberto so he wouldn't get under his feet.
Roberto couldn't budge a pumpkin and got in Marko's way.
And Marko would get angry, lunge at him, knock him down onto the prickly pumpkin leaves and vines, until one of the adults came by and pulled them apart.

Then Roberto would go and gaze for a long time into the large late pumpkin flowers, which were no longer fated to give birth to anything — inside the flowers it was yellow, cozy and smelled of pollen.
They piled the pumpkins on the grass in the yard.
There were exactly two hundred and thirty-two of them.
About another hundred were rejected for size, color and shape — they weren't sufficiently perfect for the porches of newly converted French Halloween enthusiasts.
Fumitaka coordinated the stacking of the pumpkins, and the pile came out not in the traditional, haphazardly thrown-together way, but in a beautiful geometric pyramidal shape.
Marta, resting beside the children, who grunted as they vainly tried to lift their heavy, cheek-propped, swollen eyelids, watched Fumitaka's zeal with a tenderness strange to herself.
And she thought she'd feed the children at least until they were a month old, and after that let Fumitaka deal with them.
The enormous orange pyramid shone over the whole yard, over the whole neighborhood.
The glow from it rose up into the sky.

Lilichka photographed everyone by the pumpkins in turn, while the grandmother sat by the pyramid imperturbably and watched the children scurry about, the girls run around, Nati ceaselessly chirping something into Maria's ear.
And as the pumpkins were being rolled over, a feeling came over Maria that she had practically already become the one in charge here.
This made her feel frightened, uncomfortable and bitter — but only for a moment.

Then, watching everyone run and talk, and listening with one ear to Nati's stories, an acceptance of her role suddenly descended on her like a heavenly blessing, and she didn't go to ask her own mother where to pile the pumpkins, but confidently and clearly showed Fumitaka herself where to start building the pumpkin pyramid.
Grandmother Teodora, of course, noticed this and said nothing, although she herself was convinced that the pyramid ought to have been built three meters farther to the southeast.
Children ran around her, Marko pulled Roberto's hair, Lusya with her bruised cheekbone had finally torn off all the pumpkin flowers and watched sadly as they rapidly wilted.

Marta was the first to notice something was wrong — the grandmother was supposed to be driving the flies off the newborn apostles, but she sat in the evening sun imperturbably, while the children at her feet waved their own arms when autumn flies landed on their cheeks.
A commotion broke out, someone ran about, someone phoned somewhere, someone tipped the contents of the whole first-aid kit onto the grass.
Nati prayed, Fumitaka examined the grandmother without any unnecessary movements.
Miyechka stood, caught off guard, by the apple tree that, in the grandmother's opinion, was about to topple over any moment, and waited for the grandmother's soul to rise above the pumpkin glow.
But she noticed nothing of the kind.
Teodora
'Blessed are the undefiled, who walk in the law of the Lord,' rang out from the large room where, not long ago, the ceiling had leaked.
Where Miyechka, Lilichka and Marta had quarreled over dolls, where they sewed them pencil skirts, because they couldn't do anything else.
Where Ginger had slept on the arm of the sofa before going away forever.
Where Lusya had slept during the day, that Wednesday when Ivan first brought her here.
Now the lifeless Teodora lay there, and over her stood the priest, saying: 'In the way of Thy testimonies I have delighted, as in the greatest riches…'
Marta was pale, as always.
She refused to put her hair up, although Mrs. Darochka, who was running all the proceedings, insisted on it.
— Would grandmother even have wanted this?
— she asked Maria.
— All these ceremonies?
'Thou hast rebuked the proud, the cursed, who turn aside from Thy commandments…' the priest said.
— Not that she'd have wanted it.
But she asked us to do it as is customary.
— And is this really how it's customary?
— asked Marta.
She hadn't slept that night.

Nor had Lilichka and Miyechka slept, who lay nestled against each other and listened to someone in the large room of their childhood reading and reading in a monotone voice about the Last Judgment, about humbling, and about something else that couldn't be made out.

Nati, dressed from head to toe in black, even with a black clip in her hair, sat all night beside Teodora, insisting that otherwise it wasn't done among her people and that she would do as was customary.
Maria tried to relieve her.
And even then, on this night of passage from one life into another, Nati said:
— Maria, he was very curly!
— she was getting ready to tell another story.
And Maria, that night, was strangely glad of it and listened with interest about Nati's curly-haired suitor and then about another one, who'd died a natural death.
It seemed to her that then life went on.
They whispered between themselves, while the old woman invited for the occasion, who couldn't pronounce half her letters, read about humbling, turning the pages one after another.
Nati kept watch that the old woman didn't skip a single page, and the old woman knew it.
She did her best.
Then Nati relaxed and could retell one more romantic story — short, like a flash.
It began at the Madrid metro station Las Tablas and ended at the Hospital Infanta Sofía station.
Danylo said he wouldn't have time to come.
And Miyechka convinced him there was no need anyway.
But Ustym came, although Miyechka had told him too that he shouldn't.
He appeared that same evening.
She didn't drive out to the highway, but went out to the gate.
He hugged her, and she immediately began to speak:
— Grandmother, as always, said not to brew her coffee, and then simply stopped driving the flies off the children.
— It's a good death, Miyechka, — said Ustym.
— Can it be good?
— she asked.
— It can be bad, — he answered.
Ustym took on the coordinating of the funeral simply because he understood that it would be hard for Miyechka, Lilichka and Maria.
Marta had the children, and Greg and Fumitaka understood nothing about the rites.
Nati understood the rites, but only her own, and observed only them.
She endlessly burned candles and had bought several armfuls of roses.
Ustym's only support in the organizing was Mrs. Darochka.
Some people came.
Ivan arrived, taking Lusya in his arms.
Lilichka wanted to talk with him.
She dragged him into the pantry, where, standing between sacks of flour and sugar, they whispered, until Maria looked in and said that lunch would begin soon.
From the conversation between the sacks one thing became clear — the girl was staying with Lilya.
Maria believed that she should have asked where to pile the pumpkins, then the grandmother would have lived a while longer.
To which Marta told her that this was incredible nonsense and that pumpkins weren't what had kept the grandmother in the world.
— It's a pity she didn't live to see Pavlo and Petro's eyes open, — said Marta.
— And you go on at me about pumpkins.
The children sat quietly or ran about now and then.
Sometimes the boys, together with Lusya, made an attempt to climb the pumpkin pyramid, and they were chased off it.
Everyone carried the twins in turn.
Marta's wrist hurt, and Fumitaka had only two hands.
Some people came: mustachioed women, pot-bellied men; old ones, younger ones, beautiful ones with blue eyes and bright faces, and ones already faded and elderly.
They all took turns questioning Ustym about whose children were whose, which relatives were which.
He invariably repeated:
— Ask Miyechka, ask Miyechka.
And she, for some reason, was forced to keep explaining who here was what to whom.
As soon as she finished, she'd again hear Ustym's:
— Ask Miyechka.
And again she'd explain whose twins they were, whose husbands, whether she was married.
They set the table to the southeast of the pumpkins, where the grandmother would have wanted to pile them.
Everyone sat at the table, the priest again read: 'Let Thy mercy come upon me, O Lord, Thy salvation according to Thy word.
And I shall answer those who reproach me, for I trust in Thy words…'
He drew out the sounds and read these words as if he no longer needed to think about their meaning.
The departed Teodora was mentioned only in whispers, because Nati believed that if you talked about her too much in the first days after death, her soul wouldn't be able to reunite with the Lord.
That was precisely why, on that night of prayer, she'd told Maria her stories, and possibly also because she couldn't and didn't know how to do otherwise anyway.

The priest read, people ate and talked about everyday things and trivially recalled the deceased, though truly only those who had lived with her, whom she had nursed, fed and scolded, could remember her.
No one, apart from the closest, knew or understood the significance of this house that stood amid the windfall.
She often said: 'What have I done here, having spent my whole life in a struggle with the bushes?'
— knowing in fact that she had built a refuge where you could hide the children from what you cannot influence, and then in the same way shelter their children from life.
Because life cannot always be lived, sometimes you have to hide from it.
The twins had been in the arms of most of the guests, and one of them — it was impossible to tell them apart — had just ended up with Ustym.
To Ustym's right sat an old woman with blue, slightly clouded eyes, a wrinkled face, but with cheeks smooth and firm.
To his left sat a relative from some branch — none of the girls, nor Maria, knew who he was.
He wore a gray summer cap and a pale lemon shirt.

Miyechka sat across from Ustym, the priest was saying his 'their heart is gross like curds from milk,' she looked at him, 'but I delight in Thy law,' and then she leaned across the table, catching a glass with the end of her black linen scarf, and said with her lips only: 'I'll stay with you, may I?'
'…had been my delight, I would have perished in my affliction.'
He held one of the apostles in his arms.
The baby smacked its lips.
Ustym was terribly handsome, bewildered and tired.
He simply nodded.

Lilichka did not go to Australia and took Lusya, who'd had time to grow attached to Marko and Roberto, home with her, where, together with the black-haired children with fluffy lashes, she was sent to school.
Nati picked up the children every evening — one dark-haired and two fair.

Marta tried to bring up the agreement and the short match, but became Lusya's favorite aunt, taking on the guardianship of the refuge for losers, so that at any time anyone on the threshold of catastrophe could wait out their trouble here.
Marta relieved Maria of the duty of being the eldest, and she, twenty years younger now, would come to that house as if she were Marta's daughter and not her aunt.
Fumitaka grew plums.

Danylo, following the popular millennial concept, after his breakup with Miyechka did 'something crazy' — moved to Argentina, where a dizzying corporate career caught up with him anyway, and he folded his socks every time after the wash.
The pumpkins were sold for forty-three euros apiece, earning almost eight thousand and investing it in the plum orchard.
Fumitaka insisted on documenting everyone's share.
Yulya's front paws grew fur too.
Ginger did not return.

Lilichka's photographs of the pumpkins at all stages of their ripening, together with Marta's belly, came out as a special art project in a prestigious magazine, after which an exhibition was organized on one of the capital's streets.
There came the pregnant Miyechka, who was just then carrying a curly-haired girl for Ustym, but that the girl was curly-haired only became known a year later.
The central photograph at the exhibition, in a three-by-two format, was the grandmother, who sat on a chair against the backdrop of the radiant pumpkin pyramid on the last day of her life.
[1] An informal, somewhat derogatory name for Baptists.
[2] Leave me alone (Spanish).
[3] Sweetheart (Spanish).
[4] Pretty?
(Spanish)
[5] In every sense, woman!
(Spanish)
